coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate by surface-tailored carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Wenyuan
Li, Degang
Zhang, Manqi
Ximin, Hu
Duan, Xiaoguang
Liu, Shaomin
Wang, Shaobin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a low-cost and scalable approach and the oxygen functional groups were fine-tuned by chemical post-treatment. It was found that the CQDs could be applied as visible-light-responsive photocatalysts for activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and remediation of aqueous organic dyes. Phenylhydrazine modified CQDs (CQDs-PH) presented high efficiency for degradation of methylene blue due to selective removal of carboxylic groups and inhibited recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The effects of catalyst dosage, species and concentrations of dyes, and initial pH values on the photodegradation efficiency were systematically investigated and the alkaline condition facilitates the separation of photoinduced charge carriers and promotes the dye decoloration. The reactive oxygen species produced in the photocatalysis were identified by radical quenching tests and the mechanism was elucidated. Superoxide radicals were generated from PMS activation via electron transfer from CQDs and played the primary role in organic oxidation. In addition, photogenerated holes on the valence band of CQDs also participated in the dye decomposition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Peroxymonosulfate
Superoxide
Radicals
Oxygen groups

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs and their derivatives
Degradation of organic dye compounds by the CQDs/PMS systems
Effect of CQDs derivative/PMS system on degradation of MB
Mechanism of PMS activation and MB oxidation by CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of maltitol and CQDs. (b) The survey XPS spectrum, (c) high-resolution C1s and (d) O1s XPS spectra of CQDs.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) TEM images and particle size distribution (inset), and (d) HRTEM images of the as-prepared CQDs.

(a) Emission properties of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs (inset: photographs of the sample excited by daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp). (C) Corresponding plots of (αhν)2 vs band-gap energy (hν) of CQDs.

a) Time-dependent degradation of MB by PMS in the absence and presence of CQDs. b) Effect of varying concentrations of CQDs on MB degradation in PMS/CQDs system under LED lights. c) Degradation of different concentrations of MB by the CQDs/PMS system under LED lights. d) Repeated degradation of MB by CQDs/PMS under LED light. ([MB]0 = 10 mg/L; [CQDs] = 4 g/L; [PMS]0 = 0.246 g/L).

a) Pseudo-first-order rate constants (k) of degradation of various organic dyes by the CQDs/PMS system under LED lights. b) Time-dependent degradation of TC-HCl by PMS in the absence and presence of CQDs under LED lights. ([Methylene blue] = [Rhodamine B] = [Methyl orange] = [Indigo carmine] = [TC-HCl] = 10 mg/L; [CQDs] = 4 g/L; [PMS] = 0.246 g/L) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Performances of pristine CQDs and CQDs derivatives in photocatalytic PMS activation. (b) PL spectra for CQDs and CQDs derivatives at 420 nm excitation ([MB]0 = 10 mg/L; [CQDs] = [CQDs-PH] = [CQDs-BA] = [CQDs-BrPE] = 4 g/L; [PMS]0 = 0.246 g/L).

(a), (c) Effects of various quenching agents on MB degradation. (b), (d) Effects of various quenching agents on PMS decomposition under light irradiation. ([MB] = 10 mg/L, [CQDs] = 4 g/L, [PMS] = 0.4 mM, [MA] = [TBA] = 400 mM, [AMO] = [EDTA-2Na] = 80 mM, [BQ] = [His] =20 mM, [SN] = 4 mM, [TMP] = 4, 1, and 0.4 mM, which corresponds to a molar ratio of 10, 2.5, and 1 of TMP to PMS, respectively.).

(a) Effect of the initial pH on MB degradation by PMS or CQDs/PMS systems under light irradiation. (b) PL spectra of CQDs under 420 nm excitation at different pH values and inset: variation in the PL intensity with the pH value. (The pH value was adjusted by NaOH or H2SO4 solutions).
Proposed mechanism for MB degradation via CQDs/PMS process.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate by surface-tailored carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials, The University of Adelaide, Adelaide, SA 5005, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a low-cost and scalable approach and the oxygen functional groups were fine-tuned by chemical post-treatment. It was found that the CQDs could be applied as visible-light-responsive photocatalysts for activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and remediation of aqueous organic dyes. Phenylhydrazine modified CQDs (CQDs-PH) presented high efficiency for degradation of methylene blue due to selective removal of carboxylic groups and inhibited recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The effects of catalyst dosage, species and concentrations of dyes, and initial pH values on the photodegradation efficiency were systematically investigated and the alkaline condition facilitates the separation of photoinduced charge carriers and promotes the dye decoloration. The reactive oxygen species produced in the photocatalysis were identified by radical quenching tests and the mechanism was elucidated. Superoxide radicals were generated from PMS activation via electron transfer from CQDs and played the primary role in organic oxidation. In addition, photogenerated holes on the valence band of CQDs also participated in the dye decomposition.

ce:section.ce:para:
Organic dyes are extensively used in industrial manufacture and bring vibrant colors to our modern life. However, the dye containing wastewater discharged from these factories are hazardous to the living organisms and cannot be simply treated by conventional wastewater treatment plants. In this regard, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) stand out as a great solution, leveraging the highly reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced from photocatalysis, electrochemistry and heterogeneous/homogeneous catalysis to completely mineralize the organic dyes into harmless fragments and inorganic substances (Pera-Titus et al., 2004; Oturan and Aaron, 2014). Fenton reactions apply ferrous salt (Fe2+) to reduce H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (
OH) with a high redox potential (1.9–2.7 V) to attack micropollutants via diverse routes (e.g. hydrogen abstraction, addition reaction, and electron transfer processes) (He et al., 2016; Neyens and Baeyens, 2003). Nonetheless, Fenton processes suffer from the unstable reagents for transport and storage, the sluggish kinetics for the recovery of Fe2+ from its high valence states (Fe3+/Fe4+), the demanding pH requirement (pH = ∼ 3), as well as the generation of huge amounts of iron sludge for post-treatment. Sulfate radical-based AOPs (SR-AOPs) are alternative approaches to Fenton reactions and have recently attracted soaring interests in water purification (Hu and Long, 2016; Ike et al., 2018; Oh et al., 2016).

Persulfates, such as peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and peroxydisulfate (PDS), can be activated to produce sulfate radicals (SO4
−), which exhibit a higher oxidative capacity (2.5–3.1 V) than hydroxyl radicals (1.8–2.7 V) and are adaptable to a wider pH range. Transition metal ions and oxides can effectively activate persulfates via an electron-transport process mediated by the variable-valence metal sites (Co, Mn, Fe, Cu etc.) (Anipsitakis and Dionysiou, 2004; Zhu et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2017). Recently, carbonaceous materials emerge as green catalysts for persulfates activation. The catalytic activity of nanocarbons is determined by the graphitic degree, oxygen moieties (especially electron-rich kenotic groups) and structural defects (such as topological sites, edges and vacancies) of the carbocatalysts (Sun et al., 2012; Duan et al., 2016a; Liu et al., 2016; Duan et al., 2016b). Further decorating carbon materials with metal- (Fe, Co) or metal-free (B, N, P, S) atoms can optimize the local electronic structure of sp2 carbons to boost the carbocatalysis (Duan et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Oh et al., 2017). Functionalized graphitic nanocarbons such as graphene and carbon nanotubes (single-walled or multi-walled) demonstrated high performance in catalytic oxidation, whereas their practical application is still limited by the high cost of nanomaterials fabrication, unsatisfactory stability in highly oxidative environment as well as intensive chemical inputs (the oxidants). Synthesis of high-performance and mass-producible carbocatalysts and development of novel systems by coupling carbocatalysis with external stimulators such as solar light irradiation may provide a solution to the bottlenecks.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon materials downsized to tens to hundreds of carbon atoms assembled in amorphous or crystalline quasi-spherical nanostructures. Due to the quantum size effect, CQDs normally present intriguing electronic and physicochemical features, among which the fluorescence property is most attractive to the materials community tailored by surface functionalization or passivation (Lim et al., 2015). CQDs can be synthesized via both top-down and bottom-up technologies by decomposing the bulk/nanoscale carbon materials and thermal polymerization of carbon-rich small molecules, respectively (Wu et al., 2015; Wang and Hu, 2014). However, most synthesis techniques are normally involved complicated procedures and require costly steps for separation and purification of the products. Therefore, the development of a facile strategy for large-scale production of CQDs is promising using low-cost precursors and a simple procedure.

In this study, a one-pot strategy was applied to successfully prepare high-quality CQDs in a large scale via thermal pyrolysis. The crystal structure and surface chemistry of CQDs were unveiled by the advanced characterizations. The CQDs were further applied as quantum-sized carbocatalysts for activation of PMS and remediation of a wide array of dye pollutants under LED light irradiation. In addition, surface modification with specific chemical screening agents was employed to investigate the roles of each oxygen species in photocatalysis. The impacts of operational parameters and solution pH were investigated and the primary reactive oxygen species (ROS) were identified by diverse radical scavengers. As a result, CQDs were found to be excellent visible-light-responsive photocatalysts to activate PMS to evolve free radicals and oxidative holes for degradation of dyes in the water.

Maltitol and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) aqueous solution in an analytical grade were obtained from Aladdin Reagent. Peroxymonosulfate (PMS), potassium iodide (KI), Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (RhB), indigo carmine (IC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phenylhydrazine (PH, 99 %), benzoic anhydride (BA, 98 %), 2-bromo-1-phenylethanone (BrPE, 98 %), methanol (MA, 99 %), tert-butanol (TBA, 99 %), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 99 %), ammonium oxalate (AMO, 99 %), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 99 %), silver nitrate (SN, 99 %), l-histidine (His, 98 %) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TMP, 98 %) were purchased from Adamas Reagent. Trichloromethane was purchased from McLean Reagent and was used as received. Deionized water (18.25 MΩ·cm) was used for synthesis and experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: First, a mixture of maltitol (10 g) and hydrogen peroxide solution (14 mL) were placed in a 100 mL beaker and sonicated for 3 min of dissolution process. The solution was then transferred to a glass petri dish and placed in a blast drying oven at 200 °C for 20 min to obtain roughly 8.34 g reddish brown solid carbon dots (CQDs). In this process, all H2O2 was consumed and the generated water was volatilized. The obtained solid was placed in distilled water to prepare 0.2 g/L CQDs solution. There were no aggregated precipitates after the solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min, indicating that no large particles were formed during the preparation.

For passivation of the specific oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQDs, the as-prepared CQDs were reacted with PH, BA, and BrPE to selectively block ketonic (CO), hydroxyl (OH) and carboxylic (COOH) functionalities, respectively (Cheng et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2015). The detailed preparation procedures of CQDS derivatives are described in Text S1 and Scheme S1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by a Kratos Axis UltraDLD spectrometer using monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer (US) using a KBr disc method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles of the carbon materials were obtained from the Brucker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (Germany). Raman spectra were derived from a LabRAM HR800 spectrometer (France). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were obtained on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM (US) operating at 200 kV. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450PC spectrophotometer (Japan). Photoluminescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-4600 luminescence spectrometer (Japan).

A 40 W light-emitting diode (LED) lamp was positioned over a 500 mL double cylindrical vessel, which was circulated with a condenser to maintain constant reaction temperature (25 °C). A certain amount of CQDs was homogenously dissolved in 100 mL MB solution. The distance between the LED light and suspension surface was 10 cm. The solution mixture was firstly stirred for 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium between MB and photocatalyst in dark. Then, the reaction system was continuously stirred under visible light irradiation after the addition of PMS (0.246 g/L). The concentration of MB was determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 664 nm. The concentration of PMS was measured by a spectrophotometric method using potassium iodide, and the details were presented in Text S2. Triplicate experiments were carried out and the standard deviation data were presented (Liang et al., 2008; Lee et al., 2015).

In FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 a), the stretching frequencies at 3418, 2935, 1730, 1638, 1425, 1040 and 918 cm−1 are ascribed to OH, CH, CO, CC, COC, and CO, respectively. The existence of these functional groups is beneficial to attain excellent hydrophilicity of the synthesized carbon dots. XPS measurements were performed to further analyze the composition and chemical state of the elements on the CQDs surface. As seen in Fig. 1b, two sharp peaks of C1s and O1s appear at 286.3 and 531.8 eV, respectively. Based on the calculation of peak areas, atomic ratio of C : O is 57.6 : 42.4, indicating that the CQDs contains a high level of oxygen, which is similar to the previous reports (Sahu et al., 2012; Li et al., 2011). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s was deconvoluted into five peaks of different carbon species centered at 284.4, 285.9, 286.3, 287.3, and 288.8 eV in Fig. 1c, corresponding to sp2 (CC), sp3 (CC), OH/COC, CO and COOH, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1d) shows four peaks at 531.9, 532.4, 532.9, and 534.2 eV, corresponding to CO, COOH, OH/COC and H2Oabs, respectively, which are consistent with the FTIR results. The functional groups impart the pristine CQDs excellent water solubility and potential for further functionalization.

XRD pattern of CQDs (Fig. 2 a) manifests a dominant peak at 19.4° which can be assigned to the (002) reflection of carbon. The d-spacing of CQDs (0.46 nm) is higher than that of the graphitic carbon layers (0.33 nm) with a poor crystallinity, attributed to the generation of excessive oxygen containing groups at the edging sites and between the CQDs layers (Peng et al., 2012). Raman spectrum in Fig. 2b reveals a D band at 1354 cm−1 and a G band at 1595 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered and graphitic carbons, respectively (Mewada et al., 2014). The intensity ratio of the D band and G band (ID/IG) is 0.67 due to the disturbance of the oxygen groups which caused partial sp3 hybridizations and lattice strains. HRTEM image in Fig. 2c clearly reveal that the well dispersed CQDs have a relatively monodisperse size distribution between 2.5 and 5.0 nm with a mean particle diameter of 4.1 nm. Well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm can be clearly spotted in Fig. 2d, which corresponds to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene (Dong et al., 2010).

For the optical properties of CQDs, photoluminescence measurements (Fig. 3 a) were carried out under different excitation wavelengths. The PL spectra show the strongest emission peak at 452 nm following by the excitation at 360 nm. With the increased excitation wavelength from 300 to 500 nm, the emission peak shifts from ca. 380 to ca. 540 nm, which is similar to previous studies (Jia et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014). The excitation-dependent PL behavior may be determined by the surface chemical states arisen from different functional groups (CO, COC, COH and COOH) (Zhai et al., 2012). Since the surface energy levels of CQDs are different at π and π* states, the band transition of the conjugated π domains under illumination leads to the florescence emissions. Different functional groups may generate diverse emissive traps under certain excitation wavelengths, thus presenting the excitation-dependent PL shift (Jia et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013). The CQDs exhibit bright green luminescence under 365 nm UV light illumination (Fig. 3b inset). The narrow absorption peak at 283 nm is ascribed to n–π* transition of CO band and π–π* transition of CC bonds (Jia et al., 2012; Ding et al., 2016). The optical band gap energies of the as-prepared CQDs were calculated based on the Kubelka-Munk theory using the following equation
( α h
ν )
2
= k ( h ν -
E g
)
where α, h ,
ν, k, and Eg are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, a constant and band gap energy, respectively. When the energy of photons is or greater than the band gap of CQDs, electrons are excited from the top of valence band and injected into the bottom of conduction band. The absorption of CQDs rapidly increased at 310 nm with a corresponding band gap of 3.94 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of organic dye compounds by the CQDs/PMS systems
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows MB degradation under different conditions. CQDs or PMS alone could degrade MB with only 25.4 % or 31.7 % MB removals in 60 min accordingly under visible light irradiation. When CQDs and PMS were simultaneously added into the reaction system in dark, only a small fraction of MB (19.6 %) was degraded, indicating that CQDs itself cannot serve as a carbocatalyst to directly activate PMS to generate reactive oxygen species, implying that the function of zero-dimensional (0D) CQDs is different from other dimensional nanocarbons such as 1D carbon nanotubes (single- or multi-walled), 2D graphene, and 3D porous carbons (Indrawirawan et al., 2015; Duan et al., 2015). The poor carbocatalytic activity may be due to the ultrahigh loading of oxygen groups in CQDs (up to 42.37 at.%), giving rise to a poor graphitic degree and conductivity, which is not beneficial for the direct electron transfer to PMS to produce sulfate (or hydroxyl) radicals or coordinate a nonradical electron-mediation process over the carbon network (Chen et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018). In contrast, the removal rate of MB was significantly improved and 94.3 % of MB can be rapidly oxidized in the CQDs/PMS system under LED illumination.

The effect of CQDs dosage on MB degradation is shown in Fig. 4b. As the concentration of CQDs increases, MB adsorption increased slightly (less than 10 %), while the removal rate of MB is significantly boosted. On the one hand, because of the large specific surface area of CQDs, MB can be adsorbed on the surface of carbon quantum dots, which is conducive to the photodegradation process. On the other hand, the increased loading of CQDs also speeds up the photooxidation by providing more reactive sites. When the CQDs dosage is 4 g/L, 94.3 % MB is removed in 60 min. Thus, the optimal dosage was used for the subsequent kinetic and mechanistic investigations to avoid the excessive adsorption or light screening during the photocatalysis. The effect of MB concentration was also investigated. When MB concentration was increased from 20 to 30, 40 and 50 mg/L, the degradation efficiencies were decreased from 90.1% to 81.2%, 70.7 % and 64 % (Fig. 4c), respectively, due to the insufficient ROS and competitive reaction with the decolorized intermediates.

To examine the reusability of CQDs, cyclic tests of CQDs/PMS system for photodegradation were performed. As shown in Fig. 4d, the integrated system exhibited excellent reusability and the MB degradation only decreased by 23 % after six successive runs, indicating the great stability of the photocatalyst. Notably, the carbon-based AOP systems normally suffer from poor stability, due to the adsorptive coverage of active sites by the oxidized products as well as the lattice corrosion by the highly oxidizing ROS (Li et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2017). Since CQDs possessed excellent water solubility, the hydrophilic nature of the CQDs surface alleviated the accumulation of aromatic intermediates, hereby preventing the loss/coverage of surface active sites.

The catalyst system was also evaluated for degradation of different organic dyes and the pseudo-first-order rate constants were depicted in Fig. 5 a. Intriguingly, the CQDs/PMS system manifested a particular selectivity toward the organic substrates. The degradation rates of the anionic acid dyes (methyl orange and indigo carmine) is much higher than those of cationic basic dyes (methylene blue and rhodamine B). The selectivity of the target organics may be dependent on the oxidative capacity of the produced ROS, which will be discussed in the later section.

To exclude the dye sensitization effect, TC-HCl as a typical colorless antibiotic was tested under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 5b, CQDs or PMS alone removes 21.9 % or 32.5 % of TC-HCl accordingly in 60 min under visible light irradiation. However, 96.6 % of TC-HCl is rapidly oxidized in the CQDs/PMS system under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs derivative/PMS system on degradation of MB
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of CQDs and the passivation derivatives demonstrate that the surface modification with PH, BA, and BrPE did not change the particle size and quantum yields of CQDs (Fig. S2 and Table S1). The PL (Fig. S3) and UV–vis (Fig. S4) spectra of CQDs derivatives were different from the pristine CQDs due to the varying surface chemistry tailored by the titration reaction (Zheng et al., 2011; Zhou et al., 2017). The FT-IR spectra of CQDs and CQDs derivatives are shown in Fig. S5. An obvious vibration band at 1849 cm−1 in sample CQDs-pH can be assigned to the formation of CN bonds whilst reacting with PH (Qi et al., 2013). For CQDs-BA, the intensity of the vibration peak at 1723 cm−1 (CO) is significantly increased due to the reaction with BA. A small peak at 1772 cm−1 could be observed in the FT-IR spectra for both CQDs-BA and CQDs-BrPE owing to the formation of ester groups (Sun et al., 2015). Thus, the FT-IR analysis also validated the selective removal of the targeted oxygen functional groups (Fig. S5).

As seen in Fig. 6 a, CQDs-pH exhibited a significantly improved activity compared to pristine CQDs, but the activities of CQDs-BA and CQDs-BrPE were slightly lower than that of pristine CQDs. To explore the reaction mechanism, we investigated the separation rate of photoelectron-hole pairs over different CQDs by photoluminescence spectra. Normally, a higher intensity of PL spectra would imply the intense and rapid recombination of the charge carriers to convert the adsorbed energy into photoluminescence without participating in chemical reactions (Liu et al., 2018). In Fig. 6b, at λex = 420 nm, the PL intensity of CQDs-pH at 490 nm is significantly lower than that of other CQDs. The introduction of pH inhibits the direct charge recombination, thereby promoting the segregation of charge carriers generated in the photoexcited CQDs, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic reactions. Contrarily, the peak intensities of CQDs-BA and CQDs-BrPE are slightly higher than CQDs, suggesting that the elimination of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups may promote the electron-hole recombination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of PMS activation and MB oxidation by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Various radical scavengers were introduced to the CQDs/PMS system to identify the intrinsic ROS accounting for the MB removal. Methanol (MA) would rapidly react with both sulfate radicals (SO4
−) and hydroxyl radicals(
OH) whereas tert-butanol (TBA) is a specific scavenger for
OH. p-Benzoquinone (BQ) can selectively scavenge superoxide radicals (O2
−). Silver nitrate (SN) is used as electron (e−) sacrificial agent, while ammonium oxalate (AMO) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) are scavengers for the oxidative hole (h +). Additionally, l-histidine (His) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TMP) as the scavengers of singlet oxygen (1O2) (Lee et al., 2016; Fan et al., 2019). The results in Fig. 7 a show that both TBA and MA exhibited negligible inhibitive effects on MB degradation, suggesting that both
OH and SO4
− are not the primary ROS. In contrast, the presence of p-BQ significantly reduced the MB removal rate from 94.3% to 59.5% (Fig. 7a, left), indicating that O2
− might contribute a great portion to the MB oxidation. Simultaneously, the addition of SN slightly decreased the MB removal rate from 94.3% to 82.3%, indicating that the addition of SN could consume the photo-generated electron and slow down the efficiency of CQDs/PMS degradation of MB. However, the consumption of photo-generated electrons reduced the recombination of electrons and holes, thereby improving the capacity of holes to oxidize MB. Therefore, the addition of SN only slightly inhibited the degradation of MB by CQDs/PMS. Intriguingly, the addition of AMO slightly inhibited MB degradation in the first 10 min due to h + quenching, whereas the MB oxidation was speeded up afterward; this is possibly because the accumulated electrons may assist to activate PMS to generate more active ROS via one-electron transfer, thus accelerating the MB degradation. In contrast, the effect of EDTA-2Na is different from AMO. EDTA-2Na as a hole consumer inhibited the MB degradation via rapid and direct decomposition of PMS (Fig. 7b, pink line). Therefore, EDTA-2Na might not be the perfect hole-trapping agent in the CQDs/PMS system.

Nevertheless, His and TMP as singlet oxygen scavengers caused dramatic decline in MB removals in Fig. 7a (right). Fig. 7b indicates that the two reagents directly consumed PMS even without the presence of MB (similar to EDTA-2Na). Therefore, it is ambiguous if 1O2 accounts for the MB degradation. In order to identity the role of 1O2, MB degradation was conducted in a CQDs/PMS/D2O system because the 1O2 can survive much longer in D2O (20 μs) than in H2O (2 μs), hereby enhancing the singlet oxygenation (Yun et al., 2018; Ahn et al., 2019). However, there is no obvious difference in MB degradation when the solvent was exchanged from H2O to D2O (Fig. 7c), suggesting that 1O2 did not involve in the dye decolorization. In addition, the CQDs/PMS system exhibited the same degradation rate under nitrogen purging, implies that the dissolved oxygen did not participate in the evolution of reactive species, especially for O2
−. Therefore, in the CQDs/PMS system under LED irradiation, the primary reactive species for MB oxidation are O2
− and h + from PMS activation and excited CQDs, respectively. In addition, the effects of BQ and AMO as quenching agents on PMS decomposition were further investigated. As depict in Fig. 7d, compared with CQDs alone, the combination of AMO/BQ with CQDs enhanced PMS decomposition. The presence of MB further boosted the decay of PMS. We hypothesize that the introduction of AMO may inhibit the recombination of photoelectron-hole pairs and facilitate more photoproduced electrons to be transferred to PMS to generate O2
−. Meanwhile, the presence of MB would consume the oxidative holes to reduce the electron-hole recombination and to further promote the photocatalytic activation of PMS.

Fig. 8 a shows the effect of initial pH on MB degradation by sole PMS or CQDs/PMS systems under light irradiation. At a pH range of 5–9, PMS mainly exists in the form of
H S O
5 -
(pKa1 < 1, pKa2 = 9.4) and displays the similar performance in catalytic reactions (Deng et al., 2017; Guan et al., 2011). However, MB degradation efficiency was significantly improved at higher pH values, indicating that the alkaline environment may promote the generation of more reactive species in CQDs/PMS system. The pH-dependent PL behavior of CQDs was also investigated (Fig. 8b). The PL intensities decreased at the pH values increased from 5 to 9. The improved photocatalysis may be attributed to the variations in surface charge regulated by the protonation-deprotonation processes over the oxygen groups. The similar phenomenon was observed in another study on carboxyl-grafted carbon dots (Dong et al., 2013). The alkaline condition tailored the charge state of the oxygen functionalities and promotes the effective separation of photo-induced electrons and holes on CQDs, thereby improving the hole oxidation and PMS activation via electron transfer. Consequently, the degradation efficiency of MB was impressively enhanced due to the populated ROS from photocatalysis and PMS activation.

The possible catalytic mechanism in CQDs/PMS system is proposed in Fig. 9 . Under visible light irradiation, photo-generated electrons can activate PMS (as an electron trapping agent) to generate highly reactive O2
−. The photocatalytic generation of O2
− involves in the following processes as illustrated by Eqs. (1–4). Firstly, the CQDs can be excited by the irradiation to simultaneously produce holes and electrons. Then, PMS molecule will get one electron to produce hydrogen peroxide radicals (
H O
2
•
) and a sulfite ion. The hydrogen peroxide radicals will be further decomposed into proton and superoxide ion radicals to attack MB. The oxidative holes on the valence band of CQDs directly attack MB to further facilitate the separation of electron-hole pairs and oxidation of MB. Since Eq. 3 was more favorable at basic conditions, therefore the photooxidation was enhanced at elevated pH.
(1)
C Q D s +
h ν
→
h +
+
e -
(2)
H S O
5 -
+
e -
→
H O
2
•
+
S O
3
2 -
(3)
H O
2
•
→
H +
+
O 2
• -
(4)
D y e s +
h
+
/
O 2
• -
→
P r o d u c t s

In summary, a facile and scalable approach was developed to obtain oxygen functionalized carbon quantum dots, which was evaluated for PMS activation under light irradiation for degradation of organic dyes. The CQDs/PMS system manifested excellent photocatalytic performance and reusability. The surface chemistry of CQDs was regulated by selective screening of ketonic groups with phenylhydrazine to enhance the photocatalysis, due to the suppression of electron-hole pairs of CQDs. Moreover, the system favored an alkaline condition to tune the oxygen groups to facilitate charge-carrier separation. The mechanism of photodegradation by the CQDs/PMS system was revealed that responsible reactive oxygen species for MB degradation are O2
− and h + rather than SO4
−,
OH, or 1O2. The outcomes of this study will contribute to the development of advanced carbon quantum materials for water purification coupling with photocatalysis.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Wenyuan Han: Methodology, Writing - original draft. Degang Li: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Manqi Zhang: Investigation. Ximin Hu: Investigation. Xiaoguang Duan: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Shaomin Liu: Writing - review & editing. Shaobin Wang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122695.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
